Sub-theme name: Perceived Social Burdens and Tensions Associated with the Intervention
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of social burdens, restrictions, or tensions associated with an intervention, including concerns about conflict, exclusion, or negative social consequences.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Continuity of the Intervention and Its Benefits
Descriptor: This construct captures stakeholder perceptions of whether the intervention, its associated benefits, and the rules governing it are likely to continue over time without interruption, sudden change, or withdrawal.
This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the integrity, reliability, and good faith of those responsible for implementing the intervention.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Inclusiveness and Representation in Village Decision-Making
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of whether decision-making processes related to an intervention are inclusive, representative, and allow meaningful participation across different groups.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Fairness of Benefit Distribution Within the Village
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of whether the benefits associated with an intervention are distributed equitably among households or community members, including whether any groups are perceived to be favoured or disadvantaged.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved Household Health
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of improvements in personal or household health associated with increased access to preventative and local healthcare services, including earlier treatment, improved maternal health, and reduced severity of illness.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Reliability of Farming Conditions
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increased reliability of farming conditions, including greater confidence in seasonal timing and crop yields.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Household Income
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increases in household income resulting from expanded income-generating activities, including those enabled through access to community-based savings and credit mechanisms.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Reduced Caregiving Burden
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of reduced or redistributed caregiving responsibilities within households, and the extent to which changes in care burden alter how time is allocated.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved School Attendance and Participation
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of improved school attendance and consistent participation associated with school feeding and related educational support.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Village-Level Economic Activity
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increased village-level economic activity associated with the presence of salaried staff and expanded service provision.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved Reliability of Essential Public Services
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance, significance, and lived value of improved reliability, functionality, and sustained accessibility of essential public services - including education, healthcare, and water.

Sub-theme name: Free –  Consent is Given Voluntarily Without Coercion
Descriptor: Communities  must have the ability to decide without force, manipulation, intimidation, or undue incentives. The FPIC process should ensure that Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IPLCs) can express their views openly, free from external pressure or power imbalances. Decision-making should take place in an  environment that respects cultural norms and allows for independent   reflection.
Confidence that the community can work together to solve shared problems.
Theme database: Social Cohesion and Strengthened Social Capital (https://www.notion.so/Social-Cohesion-and-Strengthened-Social-Capital-31060bfb014e803fa898d77cdce15ae5?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Linking Social Capital: Trust in Institutions and External Actors
Descriptor: Trust and ability to engage with those in power — including NGOs, government, project implementers, and traditional leaders.
Theme database: Social Cohesion and Strengthened Social Capital (https://www.notion.so/Social-Cohesion-and-Strengthened-Social-Capital-31060bfb014e803fa898d77cdce15ae5?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Bridging Social Capital: Collaboration Across Groups
Descriptor: Relationships between different social groups (e.g. ethnicities, religions, generations, clans, or genders) that foster mutual understanding and cooperation.
Theme database: Social Cohesion and Strengthened Social Capital (https://www.notion.so/Social-Cohesion-and-Strengthened-Social-Capital-31060bfb014e803fa898d77cdce15ae5?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Bonding Social Capital: Trust and Support Within Communities
Descriptor: Strength of relationships, solidarity, and mutual aid among people with shared identities or within the same group (e.g. neighbours, clan, livelihood group).
Theme database: Social Cohesion and Strengthened Social Capital (https://www.notion.so/Social-Cohesion-and-Strengthened-Social-Capital-31060bfb014e803fa898d77cdce15ae5?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Indigenous Knowledge Supports Sustainable and Culturally-Aligned Livelihoods
Descriptor: Indigenous knowledge-based economic activities—such as traditional medicine, crafts, agroecology, and ecotourism—are supported in ethical, equitable, and sustainable ways.

Sub-theme name: Integration of Indigenous Knowledge in Formal and Informal Education Systems
Descriptor: The extent to which Indigenous knowledge, language, history, and practices are included in school curricula, local education programs, and informal learning within the community.

Sub-theme name: Protection of Indigenous Cultural and Spiritual Heritage
Descriptor: Sacred sites, cultural practices, and languages are preserved and safeguarded from destruction or exploitation.

Sub-theme name: Application of Indigenous Ecological Knowledge in Land, Climate, and Conservation Efforts
Descriptor: Indigenous land stewardship and ecological knowledge inform climate adaptation, conservation, and natural resource management strategies.

Sub-theme name: Recognition and Influence of Indigenous Knowledge Holders
Descriptor: Indigenous knowledge holders—including elders, leaders, and practitioners—are recognised as experts and decision-makers in environmental governance, land management, and cultural stewardship. Their expertise actively shapes policy, planning, and implementation processes.

Sub-theme name: Redistribution of Care and Domestic Responsibilities
Descriptor: Care work, domestic responsibilities, and decision-making within households are equitably distributed, reducing the burden on women.
Theme database: Transformations in Gender and Social Inclusion (https://www.notion.so/Transformations-in-Gender-and-Social-Inclusion-31060bfb014e8078987aea7fe16c28c6?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Involvement in Collective Action and Advocacy
Descriptor: Community members—particularly women, youth, persons with disabilities, and other historically excluded groups—organise, mobilise, and advocate for their rights, needs, and priorities. This sub-theme tracks active engagement in community campaigns, movements, or initiatives that challenge injustice, influence decision-making, and foster solidarity.
Theme database: Transformations in Gender and Social Inclusion (https://www.notion.so/Transformations-in-Gender-and-Social-Inclusion-31060bfb014e8078987aea7fe16c28c6?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Reduction in Discrimination and Harmful Norms
Descriptor: Addressing and transforming the social norms, attitudes, and behaviours that reinforce exclusion, inequality, and stigma. This sub-theme focuses on reducing discrimination based on gender, disability, ethnicity, age, or other identities, and promoting inclusive beliefs and practices that uphold dignity, equity, and respect for all.
Theme database: Transformations in Gender and Social Inclusion (https://www.notion.so/Transformations-in-Gender-and-Social-Inclusion-31060bfb014e8078987aea7fe16c28c6?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Consent  – Right to Accept, Reject, or Set Conditions
Descriptor: Communities must have the power to approve, reject, or set conditions for project activities affecting them. Consent must be an ongoing process, allowing communities to withdraw or renegotiate agreements as circumstances change. Genuine consent means that decisions are respected, and project proponents cannot proceed without clear and collective agreement from the affected community.

Sub-theme name: Informed  – Access to Full, Accurate, and Understandable Information
Descriptor: Communities must have access to all relevant details about the project, including its objectives, risks, benefits, impacts, and long-term implications. Information should be shared in culturally appropriate formats and languages, ensuring that all groups—including women, elders, and marginalized members—can understand and engage meaningfully. IPLCs must also have the opportunity to seek independent advice or verification.

Sub-theme name: Prior  – Consent is Obtained Before Project Decisions Are Made
Descriptor: Consent must be sought early in the project development process—before decisions are finalized or activities begin. Communities must be engaged at the initial stages of planning and have ample time to discuss, evaluate, and decide without being rushed. FPIC should not be applied retroactively to justify actions already taken.

This subtheme refers to the construction, rehabilitation, expansion, or upgrading of physical facilities and systems that support the delivery of essential social services, particularly in sectors such as education, health, and water. It focuses on improving the availability, functionality, and accessibility of infrastructure needed for people to obtain these services, and on creating the conditions for better service access, use, and wellbeing outcomes.
Reducing cost barriers to education, healthcare, and other essential services.
Practice domain: Community Development, Economic Development, Systems Strengthening

Descriptor: Ensuring services are available in remote and underserved areas.
Practice domain: Community Development, Economic Development, Systems Strengthening

Descriptor: Ensuring that all individuals—regardless of gender, age, disability, ethnicity, language, or social status—can access and benefit from essential services without discrimination or exclusion.
Practice domain: Community Development, Economic Development, Systems Strengthening

Descriptor: Ensuring that individuals and communities are aware of the essential services available to them—such as healthcare, education, and social protection—and understand how to access them.
Practice domain: Community Development, Economic Development, Systems Strengthening

Descriptor: Ensuring that Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities have formally provided Free, Prior, and Informed Consent before any land-use changes occur. It verifies documented, time-bound consent processes grounded in transparency, participation, and non-coercion.

Ensuring that land tenure and carbon ownership rights are legally clarified and agreed upon with IPLCs prior to validation. It safeguards customary rights and prevents future disputes over resource control.

Ensuring that a community-ratified Benefit Sharing Mechanism defines how carbon revenues flow from credit sales to households. It promotes transparency, equity, and collective decision-making in financial distribution.

Ensuring that rigorous socio-economic and High Conservation Value assessments are completed with community participation prior to implementation. It establishes an evidence-based foundation for monitoring social and ecological change.

Ensuring that social and environmental risks are identified and accompanied by community-approved mitigation strategies before implementation. It protects against displacement, elite capture, conflict, and unintended harm.

Sub-theme name: Child Protection & Support for Vulnerable Children
Descriptor: Establishing strong child protection systems to prevent abuse, neglect, exploitation, and violence against children. Ensuring support for orphaned and vulnerable children through alternative care, family strengthening, and education access.

Sub-theme name: Community Safety, Crime Prevention & Justice Services
Descriptor: The extent to which community members feel safe from violence, crime, trafficking, and exploitation, and whether they believe justice systems are accessible, fair, and responsive. This includes perceptions of personal and collective safety, access to legal support, trust in justice institutions, and confidence that wrongdoings are addressed appropriately.

Ensuring that schools are accessible, inclusive, and safe, while also improving the quality of teaching, learning outcomes, and support systems for children and youth—especially those from marginalized groups. This includes investments in trained teachers, relevant curricula, adequate learning materials, and inclusive classroom practices that enable all learners to thrive.

Preventing gender-based violence (GBV) and domestic abuse through education, community engagement, and social norm change, while ensuring that survivors have access to safe spaces, emergency care, legal aid, and long-term support. A survivor-centered approach prioritizes dignity, confidentiality, and access to justice for those affected.

Ensuring that individuals and families have access to quality, affordable, and respectful healthcare services, including mental health and psychosocial support. This includes preventive and curative care, public health interventions, and counselling services that are culturally appropriate, confidential, and responsive to community needs—particularly for those experiencing trauma, chronic illness, or emotional distress.
Expanding safety nets for individuals facing extreme poverty, displacement, or social exclusion, ensuring that vulnerable groups receive necessary support services.

Sub-theme name: Clarity about the short-term and long-term benefits of the project
Descriptor: Ensuring that all stakeholders, particularly local communities, have a clear understanding of both the immediate and long-term benefits of the project. Clarity helps to manage expectations and promote trust in the project.

Sub-theme name: Plans in place to ensure that marginalized groups benefit
Descriptor: Ensuring that benefit-sharing mechanisms are inclusive and equitable, particularly for women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, and other marginalized groups.

Sub-theme name: Potential negative unintended consequences considered & mitigated
Descriptor: Ensuring that benefit-sharing mechanisms do not inadvertently create harm, exclusion, or social and economic disparities.

Ensuring that a community-ratified Benefit Sharing Mechanism defines how carbon revenues flow from credit sales to households. It promotes transparency, equity, and collective decision-making in financial distribution.

Sub-theme name: Access to Financial Services & Asset Building
Descriptor: The extent to which individuals and households can access financial services—such as loans, savings accounts, and credit facilities—and their ability to build and sustain financial assets over time.
Access to stable, fair, and dignified employment opportunities, ensuring compliance with labor rights and equitable pay.
Changes in household income levels and overall economic well-being, assessing whether families experience increased financial security and reduced poverty.

Supporting individuals and communities in developing multiple income-generating activities to reduce economic vulnerability, increase financial stability, and enhance resilience to external shocks such as climate change, market fluctuations, and job loss.
Support for small businesses, cooperatives, and local enterprises, fostering self-reliance and new income streams.

Ensuring that rigorous socio-economic and High Conservation Value assessments are completed with community participation prior to implementation. It establishes an evidence-based foundation for monitoring social and ecological change.

Protecting and restoring biodiversity and ecosystems to sustain and enhance local livelihoods. This includes conservation of forests, wetlands, and wildlife to support sustainable agriculture, fishing, ecotourism, and other nature-based economic activities. By maintaining healthy ecosystems, communities can improve food security, climate resilience, and long-term economic opportunities while ensuring the sustainable use of natural resources.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Strengthening the ability of ecosystems to mitigate floods, droughts, and extreme weather impacts.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Strengthening the ability of ecosystems to mitigate floods, droughts, and extreme weather impacts.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Reducing pollution from agricultural runoff, plastic waste, and industrial activities to protect soil, water, and air quality. Promoting circular economy practices, waste recycling, and responsible disposal methods.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Expanding community access to clean energy sources, such as solar, wind, and bioenergy, to reduce reliance on fossil fuels, improve energy security, and support sustainable livelihoods.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Promoting land-use practices that maintain soil health, improve agricultural productivity, and support ecosystem balance. This includes preventing land degradation, enhancing soil fertility, and ensuring that land-use decisions balance environmental conservation with sustainable livelihoods. Effective land and soil management practices contribute to food security, climate resilience, and long-term economic stability for communities.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Ensuring access to clean water through forest conservation, wetland protection, and sustainable land management.
Theme database: Improved Ecosystem Services & Practices for Sustainable Livelihoods (https://www.notion.so/Improved-Ecosystem-Services-Practices-for-Sustainable-Livelihoods-31060bfb014e8034b00eef75e9399529?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Ensuring that local governance mechanisms responsible for decision-making on project activities are inclusive, representative, and effective. These structures may include formal or informal bodies such as village councils, forest user groups, Indigenous governance systems, land management committees, and other community-led institutions.
Supporting diverse leadership, including women, youth, and indigenous groups in governance structures
Ensuring that communities—including marginalized groups—have meaningful influence in shaping project design, implementation, and benefit-sharing.

Enabling individuals to access relevant information, develop critical thinking, and apply knowledge to make informed choices about their lives, livelihoods, and participation in community development. Their confidence, self-efficacy, and the ability to act independently and responsibly.

Sub-theme name: Community-Led Learning, M&E, and Knowledge Sharing
Descriptor: Building local capacity to monitor progress, reflect on results, and share knowledge across peer groups and networks. This includes the use of participatory monitoring tools, peer learning exchanges, and evidence-based planning to improve accountability, ownership, and adaptive learning.

Increasing awareness of civic rights, responsibilities, and legal entitlements, while strengthening meaningful participation in social, political, and policy processes. This includes understanding laws, speaking up in community forums, and influencing decisions that affect livelihoods, land, or services.

Enhancing the knowledge, skills, and confidence of local leaders, institutions, and governance structures to make inclusive, accountable, and transparent decisions. This includes traditional and elected leaders, youth leaders, and community committees responsible for project implementation or collective planning.
Implementing policies, bylaws and practices that prevent deforestation, land degradation, and illegal land conversion.
Enhancing legal frameworks, land tenure security, and institutional capacity to enforce forest protection policies and support community rights.
Ensuring that forests continue to provide critical ecosystem services such as water filtration, soil stabilization, and flood prevention through integrated watershed management.
Implementing community-led fire management, early warning systems, and training programmes to reduce the risk of wildfires and other climate-induced disasters affecting forests.
Supporting sustainable economic activities such as agroforestry, non-timber forest product harvesting, and ecotourism to provide income while maintaining forest integrity.
Protecting and restoring forest biodiversity, ensuring the survival of key species, and rehabilitating degraded landscapes to enhance ecological resilience.
Empowering Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities as custodians of forests
Community-led strategies to prevent illegal logging, land grabbing, wildfires, and other unauthorized activities that threaten forests, land tenure security, and biodiversity.

Sub-theme name: Prevention of Forced Labour, Child Labour, Trafficking, and Exploitation
Descriptor: Ensuring that project activities uphold human rights by preventing forced labour, child labour, human trafficking, and other forms of exploitation. This includes establishing robust policies, monitoring mechanisms, and community safeguards to protect workers—especially vulnerable groups such as children, women, Indigenous Peoples, and migrant workers

Ensuring safe and dignified working conditions for all, including adherence to occupational health and safety standards, prevention of harassment and abuse, and access to safe reporting mechanisms without fear of reprisal.

Sub-theme name: Prevention of Sexual Harassment & Gender-Based Violence
Descriptor: Ensuring a safe and respectful environment in all project activities by preventing sexual harassment and gender-based violence (GBV). Fostering a zero-tolerance culture for harassment, discrimination, and violence.

Projects promote gender equality, disability inclusion, and non-discriminatory practices in employment, leadership, and benefit-sharing. All individuals, regardless of race, ethnicity, age, gender identity, disability, or socioeconomic status, have equal opportunities to participate and benefit.
Wages and compensation structures are transparent, fair, and non-discriminatory, ensuring equal pay for equal work regardless of gender, ethnicity, disability, or other identity markers.

Staff members have participated in and successfully completed training programmes aimed at enhancing their professional skills, technical expertise, and capacity to contribute effectively to the project's objectives.
Government staff who have participated in training through the project to build their knowledge and skills in areas relevant to the project.
Project partners who have participated in training through the project to build their knowledge and skills in areas relevant to the project.
Community members who have participated in training through the project to build their knowledge and skills in areas relevant to the project.

Extent to which project stakeholders—particularly community members, local institutions, and implementing partners—have access to and demonstrate understanding of key carbon market concepts, processes, and technical requirements, including carbon accounting, credit generation, verification standards, and market mechanisms.
Publicly sharing project goals, funding, and impact assessments.
Establishing accessible and independent channels for resolving community concerns.
Defining accountability structures for project implementers, governments, and communities.
Communities' rights to access, manage, and benefit from land and resources are legally recognized and upheld.

Ensuring that the cultural heritage, traditional knowledge, and practices of Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities are respected, protected, and integrated into project design and implementation. This includes recognizing Indigenous land stewardship, preserving sacred sites, and upholding the rights of communities to maintain and transmit their cultural and ecological knowledge

Sub-theme name: Land use plans agreed – no displacement
Descriptor: Land use plans are developed and agreed upon through inclusive processes, ensuring sustainable management without displacing communities
Land ownership and usage rights are formally documented, reducing conflicts and ensuring long-term security for communities.

Covers caregiver attendance at school events, parent meetings, open days, and volunteering. Captures physical presence and active participation in the school community, distinct from communication behaviours. Grounded in Epstein's typology of parental involvement, specifically the volunteering and school involvement dimensions.

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Support for Learning at Home
Descriptor: Covers the home learning environment, homework support, reading with children, and creation of conditions conducive to study. The most direct caregiver influence on learner attainment and consistently the strongest predictor of outcomes in the parental engagement literature. Captures home-based support as a distinct and critical engagement mechanism.

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Attitudes and Beliefs about Education
Descriptor: Covers caregiver values, aspirations, and expectations for their child's education. Caregiver beliefs about the value of education are stronger predictors of learner persistence than structural engagement behaviours. Captures the attitudinal preconditions for sustained engagement rather than the behaviours themselves.

Covers structural, economic, cultural, and social barriers preventing caregivers from engaging, including work schedules, distance, gender norms, and language. A complementary diagnostic subtheme rather than a standalone outcome subtheme. Essential for interpreting low scores on any other caregiver engagement indicator and for informing programme design.

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Confidence and Capacity to Engage
Descriptor: Covers caregiver self-efficacy in supporting learning, including literacy, numeracy, and confidence to interact with school staff. Captures programme effects on caregiver empowerment as a condition for engagement, particularly relevant in low-literacy contexts where willingness to engage may exist but skills or confidence are barriers.

Covers caregiver mental health, stress levels, and personal resilience as determinants of the quality and consistency of care and learning support they can provide. A caregiver who is overwhelmed, burnt out, or experiencing chronic stress cannot engage meaningfully with their child's education regardless of their knowledge, attitudes, or skills. This subtheme captures the personal resource dimension of caregiver empowerment and is a documented upstream determinant of child learning outcomes in adverse childhood experience and family resilience literature.

Covers the degree to which caregivers feel a sense of personal agency, voice, and capacity to make informed decisions that affect their child's education and wellbeing. Distinct from task-specific confidence in homework support and from peer support participation. Captures whether caregivers see themselves as active agents in their child's educational journey rather than passive recipients of school and programme decisions. Grounded in Freire's critical pedagogy and Malhotra and Schuler's empowerment framework.

Covers the degree to which young people participate in civic processes beyond programme-structured settings, including community governance structures, local decision-making bodies, public consultations, civic advocacy, and community accountability mechanisms. Distinct from peer leadership, which is relational and peer-directed, and from community service, which is activity-based. Civic engagement captures whether young people are developing as active citizens who can navigate and influence formal civic structures. Grounded in Youniss, Flanagan, and Checkoway's civic development literature.

Sub-theme name: Recognition and Visibility of Youth Contribution
Descriptor: Covers whether youth contributions, ideas, and leadership are formally acknowledged, made visible to the broader community, and positioned as credible and valued by adults and institutions. Recognition is distinct from contribution as a behaviour and captures the structural and relational conditions that sustain youth engagement over time. Whether youth feel seen and valued as contributors is a documented predictor of civic identity development, sustained participation, and resilience against marginalisation, particularly for young people from underrepresented or excluded groups.

Covers how different actors operating in the same community or programme area, including schools, health services, social protection systems, NGOs, community structures, and government agencies, coordinate referrals and joint case management for learners and families with complex or intersecting needs. Distinct from partner alignment, which concerns strategic and evidential coordination. Cross-sector coordination and referral captures the operational interface between systems at the point of delivery.

Covers whether structured collective reflection, joint learning reviews, and shared lesson documentation practices exist and function across the programme and its partners. Distinct from evidence use and adaptation, which concerns how evidence informs decisions, and from partner alignment, which concerns strategic coordination. Captures the social and collective processes through which actors learn together from experience and embed lessons into shared practice.

Covers whether programme partners share data, jointly collect monitoring information, and coordinate data use to avoid duplication and build a shared picture of what is happening across the system. Distinct from partner alignment, which concerns strategic coordination. Captures the operational systems and practices through which monitoring information flows across actors, enabling a coherent shared evidence base rather than fragmented parallel monitoring efforts.

Covers whether programme outcomes are equitably distributed across defined equity dimensions including gender, disability, socioeconomic background, ethnicity, geographic location, and age band. A monitoring and analysis construct rather than a programme activity construct. The ultimate test of whether the programme's equity commitments translate into equitable results, requiring systematic outcome disaggregation and gap analysis.

Covers the degree to which girls, women, and gender-diverse young people participate equitably in programme activities, have equal voice in decision-making, and experience gender-equitable norms and attitudes in learning and community settings. Distinct from targeting and barrier reduction, which concerns access, and from disaggregated outcome equity, which concerns outcome distribution. Captures whether equitable participation is achieved in practice and whether gender norms are shifting.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Fairness and Sense of Belonging
Descriptor: Covers whether learners, youth, and community members from marginalised backgrounds feel they belong, are respected, treated fairly, and valued as equal participants in the programme environment. A perceptual equity construct distinct from structural access and from outcome equity. Captures the lived experience of inclusion, which is the most meaningful test of whether structural equity measures are working in practice.

Sub-theme name: Material and Physical Home Learning Conditions
Descriptor: Covers the physical and material conditions within the household that either enable or suppress a child's capacity to engage with learning at home. Distinct from caregiver behaviours, which are covered under Caregiver Support for Learning at Home. Captures the structural material infrastructure of the home environment, including study space, lighting, learning materials, and freedom from competing household demands during study time.

Covers whether teaching and facilitation methods are genuinely inclusive, accommodating diverse learning needs, styles, and abilities through differentiated instruction, adapted materials, and responsive facilitation. Distinct from Inclusion of Vulnerable Learners, which concerns who is present, and from Safety, Dignity and Respect, which concerns interpersonal treatment. Captures the instructional quality dimension of inclusion, whether learning delivery is itself accessible and responsive to all learners regardless of ability or need.

Covers whether monitoring data is routinely reviewed, discussed, and used at the team and programme level to inform operational decisions, not just collected and reported upward. An internal data use culture construct distinct from inter-partner data sharing and from strategic evidence use. Captures whether there is a functioning internal culture of data-informed decision-making among the programme team, where monitoring data is trusted, accessible, understood, and acted upon.

Covers whether programme staff and facilitators engage in continuous professional learning, peer observation, coaching, and reflective practice as a routine part of their working life within the programme. An individual and team-level professional learning construct distinct from collective partner learning, which is covered under Coordination and Collective Learning, and from evidence use, which concerns strategic decisions. Captures whether the people delivering the programme are themselves learning and improving.

Covers whether the programme is transparent and accountable to the learners, caregivers, and communities it serves, including how it communicates results, invites and responds to feedback, and handles complaints. A downward accountability construct distinct from community engagement, which concerns involvement, and from evidence use, which is internal. Captures whether the programme reports back to those it serves and responds to concerns in a timely and trustworthy way.

Covers whether the programme identifies learners who have fallen behind expected learning levels and provides structured catch-up or remediation support to close those gaps. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns ongoing learning delivery for all learners, and from attendance and enrolment continuity, which concerns structural participation. Captures the targeted diagnostic and recovery response when continuity has broken down for specific learners.

Covers whether learners remain enrolled and attending consistently across the school year and between school years, capturing the structural participation dimension of learning continuity. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns learning quality, and from learning loss identification and recovery, which concerns remediation after disruption. Attendance and enrolment continuity is the structural prerequisite for all learning progression outcomes.

Covers whether learners successfully navigate key educational transitions, including from primary to secondary, from out-of-school to enrolled, or from non-formal to formal education, and whether the programme provides structured transition support. Distinct from attendance and enrolment continuity, which concerns within-level participation, and from foundational learning experiences, which concerns within-level learning quality. Captures movement between educational levels and systems, a documented high dropout risk point.

Covers whether young people feel capable of making informed decisions about their educational and livelihood futures, have engaged in deliberate decision-making processes, and feel confident taking the steps required to pursue their chosen pathways. An agency and self-efficacy construct distinct from pathway knowledge, which is cognitive, and from institutional navigation, which is operational. Captures whether young people believe they can shape their own futures and are actively doing so.

Covers whether young people are able to access and navigate the institutions, services, and referral systems needed to pursue their chosen education and livelihood pathways, including applying to institutions, accessing financial support, and completing required enrolment processes. The operational navigation dimension distinct from pathway knowledge, which concerns what is known, and from decision-making self-efficacy, which concerns the confidence to choose. Captures whether young people can complete the practical steps between pathway decision and pathway entry.

Covers whether young people have accurate, sufficient, and accessible knowledge of the education and livelihood pathways available to them, including entry requirements, costs, timelines, application processes, and sources of support. The information precondition for informed pathway navigation. Distinct from decision-making self-efficacy, which concerns the agentic dimension, and from institutional navigation, which concerns access rather than knowledge.

Covers the positive psychological resources that enable young people to engage actively and constructively with learning and life, including hope, sense of purpose, motivation, positive self-concept, and optimism. Distinct from emotional safety and stability, which concerns freedom from threat, and from resilience and coping, which concerns managing adversity. Captures the presence of constructive psychological assets rather than the absence of distress, representing the positive pole of the psychosocial wellbeing spectrum.

Covers whether young people have positive, supportive, and trusting peer relationships within the programme environment, including friendships, peer support networks, and social belonging to the peer group. Distinct from broader institutional belonging under Equity and Inclusion and from interpersonal safety under Inclusive Learning Environments. Captures the relational dimension of psychosocial wellbeing, whether young people feel they have trusted peers in the programme context.

Covers whether the programme is actively seeking to influence policy, regulation, or government practice through evidence submission, policy consultation participation, stakeholder engagement with government as the primary audience, and documented instances of government decision-making informed by programme evidence. The directional and strategic dimension of public sector engagement. Distinct from government relationship building, which is relational, and from adoption into public systems, which is the institutional outcome.

Covers whether the programme is actively cultivating working relationships with relevant government departments, agencies, and officials, including participation in government forums, joint planning, formal engagement mechanisms, and MOUs. The relationship and process dimension of public sector engagement. Distinct from adoption into public systems, which is the institutional outcome, and from policy influence and advocacy, which concerns changing government practice through evidence.

Covers whether learners have the foundational cognitive and language skills required to engage with learning at their current level, including language of instruction proficiency, working memory and attention, and pre-literacy and pre-numeracy readiness. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns current attainment, and from disability access and support, which concerns accommodation. Captures the cognitive prerequisites that determine whether a learner can access and process learning at their current level.

Covers whether learners consistently arrive at learning sessions on time, adequately prepared, and in a state of readiness to engage. The session-level behavioural readiness dimension distinct from attendance and enrolment continuity, which captures structural participation across the year. Captures punctuality, session preparedness, and alertness at session start as conditions that determine whether enrolled and present learners can benefit from each individual learning opportunity.

Covers whether teachers and facilitators have adequate motivation, working conditions, and wellbeing to sustain high-quality instructional practice over time. The human sustainability construct for the teaching workforce. Distinct from instructional leadership, which is the institutional enabling condition, and from professional learning cultures, which is collaborative learning. Captures the individual and relational conditions that determine whether teachers can and will sustain high-quality practice.

Covers whether school leaders and programme managers are providing the instructional leadership, structured teacher support, and school improvement planning required to create the institutional conditions for sustained learning quality improvement. The top-down enabling condition for school improvement. Distinct from professional learning cultures, which is peer-driven, and from assessment for learning systems, which is classroom-level. Captures whether leaders create the structural and cultural conditions that enable all other improvement processes to function.

Covers whether schools and programmes have functioning systems for using assessment data to understand learner progress and adapt instruction, including assessment tools, data recording, analysis routines, and instructional adaptation practices. Distinct from foundational learning attainment, which measures outcomes, and from professional learning cultures, which is peer-driven. Captures the institutional feedback loop between assessment information and teaching decisions.

Covers whether learners can work effectively with others, contribute to collective tasks, resolve disagreements constructively, and participate in basic civic processes. A relational and participatory competency construct. Distinct from communication skills, which is individual expression, and from learning to learn, which is internal and metacognitive. Captures the social infrastructure for adaptability and work readiness enacted in group and community contexts.

Covers the metacognitive and self-regulatory skills that enable learners to direct their own learning, including goal setting, self-monitoring, persistence, and reflection. The foundational meta-skill construct. Distinct from domain-specific skills such as communication and digital skills, and from collaborative and civic skills. Captures the internal infrastructure that determines whether other transferable skills can be acquired and sustained across contexts.

Covers whether learners can apply skills acquired in the programme context to new or real-world situations outside the classroom, including recognising skill relevance in a new context, adapting the skill appropriately, and applying it successfully. The transfer and application construct. Distinct from skill acquisition, which concerns whether skills were learned, and from learning to learn, which is metacognitive. Captures whether programme-acquired skills produce real-world benefit beyond the learning context.

Tracks income generated by the Learning Centre through space rental, tenancy agreements, and bespoke organisational training, and the proportion of Learning Centre running costs covered by commercial income.

Tracks the number of individuals accessing free learning programmes through the Learning Centre, disaggregated by age, gender, and vulnerability status, and the volume of community learning sessions delivered directly and through tenant partners.

Captures the extent to which a scholar's participation in TFFT programmes positively influences the educational attitudes, behaviours, and aspirations of siblings, parents, and wider household members — modelling what learning makes possible and raising household aspirations.

Ensuring that all project workers receive wages and compensation that are fair, transparent, and equitable across gender and identity groups, and that employment conditions meet recognised decent work standards including job security, benefits, and dignified treatment.

Ensuring that project employment opportunities prioritise and are accessible to local community members, including women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, and other marginalised groups. This sub-theme tracks equitable recruitment practices, local hiring targets, and the removal of barriers that prevent community members from benefiting economically from project-generated employment.

Ensuring that project workers have access to functioning, accessible, and trusted mechanisms to raise concerns about working conditions, treatment, or rights violations, and that worker-management relations are governed by fair, transparent, and accountable processes.

Ensuring that project workers operate in safe and healthy working environments, receive adequate safety training before commencing work, and have access to protective equipment and emergency procedures. This sub-theme also tracks investment in worker skill development and capacity building.

Measuring the physical safety risks and psychological wellbeing impacts experienced by community members as a result of human-wildlife conflict, including fear, anxiety, trauma, and changes in daily behaviour. This sub-theme tracks whether communities feel safe and whether mental health burdens associated with conflict are addressed.

Assessing the economic losses experienced by households as a result of human-wildlife conflicts, including damage to crops, livestock, property, and stored food. This sub-theme tracks the magnitude and distribution of livelihood impacts and the extent to which affected households are able to recover.

Assessing how land-use changes within and around the project boundary affect wildlife movement patterns and the resulting displacement of wildlife into community areas, which can intensify human-wildlife conflicts. This sub-theme monitors the interaction between project conservation activities and wildlife-community interface dynamics.

Monitoring the frequency, type, location, and severity of human-wildlife conflict incidents within and around the project boundary. This includes crop raiding, livestock predation, property damage, and direct attacks on people, tracked over time to assess conflict trends and the effectiveness of mitigation measures.

Evaluating the accessibility, timeliness, adequacy, and fairness of compensation and insurance schemes for households affected by human-wildlife conflict losses. This sub-theme tracks whether affected families receive meaningful support and whether compensation mechanisms function as intended.

Ensuring that young people have formal voting rights and meaningful representation on Project Steering Committees and Benefit-Sharing Boards, transforming youth from passive beneficiaries into active decision-makers in project governance.

Supporting youth to establish and grow enterprises rooted in carbon finance and conservation activities, including nurseries, sustainable harvesting, and eco-tourism businesses funded or catalysed by carbon revenues.

Building youth capacity to use digital conservation and carbon monitoring tools including dMRV apps, drone-based mapping, and remote sensing platforms, enabling youth-led data collection and monitoring.
Developing high-level technical competency in nature-based solutions application and climate-risk assessment among project youth, tracked through standardised post-training assessments.
Enabling youth to lead and participate in formal policy submissions, community advocacy campaigns, and public engagement on carbon and climate issues at national and international levels.
Connecting project youth to direct financial benefits from carbon dividends through mobile money accounts and micro-finance products, building financial independence and inclusion.

Building the technical knowledge and certified skills of youth in nature-based solutions, ecosystem management, and climate adaptation. This sub-theme tracks participation in certification programmes and the transfer of practical NbS skills to young people.

Ensuring youth representatives are formally involved across the full project cycle — planning, monitoring, and reporting — not only as token representatives but as active contributors with defined roles and accountability.
